title: II: Confession
html:
II

Confession

Late that same afternoon, Jolyon had a nap in the old armchair. Face down on his knee was La Rotisserie de la Refine Pedauque, and just before he fell asleep he had been thinking: ‚ÄúAs a people shall we ever really like the French? Will they ever really like us!‚Äù He himself had always liked the French, feeling at home with their wit, their taste, their cooking. Irene and he had paid many visits to France before the War, when Jon had been at his private school. His romance with her had begun in ParisÔªø‚Äîhis last and most enduring romance. But the FrenchÔªø‚Äîno Englishman could like them who could not see them in some sort with the detached aesthetic eye! And with that melancholy conclusion he had nodded off.

When he woke he saw Jon standing between him and the window. The boy had evidently come in from the garden and was waiting for him to wake. Jolyon smiled, still half asleep. How nice the chap lookedÔªø‚Äîsensitive, affectionate, straight! Then his heart gave a nasty jump; and a quaking sensation overcame him. Jon! That confession! He controlled himself with an effort. ‚ÄúWhy, Jon, where did you spring from?‚Äù

Jon bent over and kissed his forehead.

Only then he noticed the look on the boy‚Äôs face.

‚ÄúI came home to tell you something, Dad.‚Äù

With all his might Jolyon tried to get the better of the jumping, gurgling sensations within his chest.

‚ÄúWell, sit down, old man. Have you seen your mother?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo.‚Äù The boy‚Äôs flushed look gave place to pallor; he sat down on the arm of the old chair, as, in old days, Jolyon himself used to sit beside his own father, installed in its recesses. Right up to the time of the rupture in their relations he had been wont to perch thereÔªø‚Äîhad he now reached such a moment with his own son? All his life he had hated scenes like poison, avoided rows, gone on his own way quietly and let others go on theirs. But nowÔªø‚Äîit seemedÔªø‚Äîat the very end of things, he had a scene before him more painful than any he had avoided. He drew a visor down over his emotion, and waited for his son to speak.

‚ÄúFather,‚Äù said Jon slowly, ‚ÄúFleur and I are engaged.‚Äù

‚ÄúExactly!‚Äù thought Jolyon, breathing with difficulty.

‚ÄúI know that you and Mother don‚Äôt like the idea. Fleur says that Mother was engaged to her father before you married her. Of course I don‚Äôt know what happened, but it must be ages ago. I‚Äôm devoted to her, Dad, and she says she is to me.‚Äù

Jolyon uttered a queer sound, half laugh, half groan.

‚ÄúYou are nineteen, Jon, and I am seventy-two. How are we to understand each other in a matter like this, eh?‚Äù

‚ÄúYou love Mother, Dad; you must know what we feel. It isn‚Äôt fair to us to let old things spoil our happiness, is it?‚Äù

Brought face to face with his confession, Jolyon resolved to do without it if by any means he could. He laid his hand on the boy‚Äôs arm.

‚ÄúLook, Jon! I might put you off with talk about your both being too young and not knowing your own minds, and all that, but you wouldn‚Äôt listen, besides, it doesn‚Äôt meet the caseÔªø‚ÄîYouth, unfortunately, cures itself. You talk lightly about ‚Äòold things like that,‚Äô knowing nothingÔªø‚Äîas you say trulyÔªø‚Äîof what happened. Now, have I ever given you reason to doubt my love for you, or my word?‚Äù

At a less anxious moment he might have been amused by the conflict his words arousedÔªø‚Äîthe boy‚Äôs eager clasp, to reassure him on these points, the dread on his face of what that reassurance would bring forth; but he could only feel grateful for the squeeze.

‚ÄúVery well, you can believe what I tell you. If you don‚Äôt give up this love affair, you will make Mother wretched to the end of her days. Believe me, my dear, the past, whatever it was, can‚Äôt be buriedÔªø‚Äîit can‚Äôt indeed.‚Äù

Jon got off the arm of the chair.

‚ÄúThe girl‚ÄùÔªø‚Äîthought JolyonÔªø‚Äî‚Äúthere she goesÔªø‚Äîstarting up before himÔªø‚Äîlife itselfÔªø‚Äîeager, pretty, loving!‚Äù

‚ÄúI can‚Äôt, Father; how can IÔªø‚Äîjust because you say that? Of course, I can‚Äôt!‚Äù

‚ÄúJon, if you knew the story you would give this up without hesitation; you would have to! Can‚Äôt you believe me?‚Äù

‚ÄúHow can you tell what I should think? Father, I love her better than anything in the world.‚Äù

Jolyon‚Äôs face twitched, and he said with painful slowness:

‚ÄúBetter than your mother, Jon?‚Äù

From the boy‚Äôs face, and his clenched fists Jolyon realised the stress and struggle he was going through.

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know,‚Äù he burst out, ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know! But to give Fleur up for nothingÔªø‚Äîfor something I don‚Äôt understand, for something that I don‚Äôt believe can really matter half so much, will make meÔªø‚Äîmake me.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúMake you feel us unjust, put a barrierÔªø‚Äîyes. But that‚Äôs better than going on with this.‚Äù

‚ÄúI can‚Äôt. Fleur loves me, and I love her. You want me to trust you; why don‚Äôt you trust me, Father? We wouldn‚Äôt want to know anythingÔªø‚Äîwe wouldn‚Äôt let it make any difference. It‚Äôll only make us both love you and Mother all the more.‚Äù

Jolyon put his hand into his breast pocket, but brought it out again empty, and sat, clucking his tongue against his teeth.

‚ÄúThink what your mother‚Äôs been to you, Jon! She has nothing but you; I shan‚Äôt last much longer.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy not? It isn‚Äôt fair toÔªø‚ÄîWhy not?‚Äù

‚ÄúWell,‚Äù said Jolyon, rather coldly, ‚Äúbecause the doctors tell me I shan‚Äôt; that‚Äôs all.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, Dad!‚Äù cried Jon, and burst into tears.

This downbreak of his son, whom he had not seen cry since he was ten, moved Jolyon terribly. He recognised to the full how fearfully soft the boy‚Äôs heart was, how much he would suffer in this business, and in life generally. And he reached out his hand helplesslyÔªø‚Äînot wishing, indeed not daring to get up.

‚ÄúDear man,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúdon‚ÄôtÔªø‚Äîor you‚Äôll make me!‚Äù

Jon smothered down his paroxysm, and stood with face averted, very still.

‚ÄúWhat now?‚Äù thought Jolyon. ‚ÄúWhat can I say to move him?‚Äù

‚ÄúBy the way, don‚Äôt speak of that to Mother,‚Äù he said; ‚Äúshe has enough to frighten her with this affair of yours. I know how you feel. But, Jon, you know her and me well enough to be sure we wouldn‚Äôt wish to spoil your happiness lightly. Why, my dear boy, we don‚Äôt care for anything but your happinessÔªø‚Äîat least, with me it‚Äôs just yours and Mother‚Äôs and with her just yours. It‚Äôs all the future for you both that‚Äôs at stake.‚Äù

Jon turned. His face was deadly pale; his eyes, deep in his head, seemed to burn.

‚ÄúWhat is it? What is it? Don‚Äôt keep me like this!‚Äù

Jolyon, who knew that he was beaten, thrust his hand again into his breast pocket, and sat for a full minute, breathing with difficulty, his eyes closed. The thought passed through his mind: ‚ÄúI‚Äôve had a good long inningsÔªø‚Äîsome pretty bitter momentsÔªø‚Äîthis is the worst!‚Äù Then he brought his hand out with the letter, and said with a sort of fatigue: ‚ÄúWell, Jon, if you hadn‚Äôt come today, I was going to send you this. I wanted to spare youÔªø‚ÄîI wanted to spare your mother and myself, but I see it‚Äôs no good. Read it, and I think I‚Äôll go into the garden.‚Äù He reached forward to get up.

Jon, who had taken the letter, said quickly, ‚ÄúNo, I‚Äôll go‚Äù; and was gone.

Jolyon sank back in his chair. A bluebottle chose that moment to come buzzing round him with a sort of fury; the sound was homely, better than nothing.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ Where had the boy gone to read his letter? The wretched letterÔªø‚Äîthe wretched story! A cruel businessÔªø‚Äîcruel to herÔªø‚Äîto SoamesÔªø‚Äîto those two childrenÔªø‚Äîto himself!Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ His heart thumped and pained him. LifeÔªø‚Äîits lovesÔªø‚Äîits workÔªø‚Äîits beautyÔªø‚Äîits aching, andÔªø‚Äîits end! A good time; a fine time in spite of all; untilÔªø‚Äîyou regretted that you had ever been born. LifeÔªø‚Äîit wore you down, yet did not make you want to dieÔªø‚Äîthat was the cunning evil! Mistake to have a heart! Again the bluebottle came buzzingÔªø‚Äîbringing in all the heat and hum and scent of summerÔªø‚Äîyes, even the scentÔªø‚Äîas of ripe fruits, dried grasses, sappy shrubs, and the vanilla breath of cows. And out there somewhere in the fragrance Jon would be reading that letter, turning and twisting its pages in his trouble, his bewilderment and troubleÔªø‚Äîbreaking his heart about it! The thought made Jolyon acutely miserable. Jon was such a tenderhearted chap, affectionate to his bones, and conscientious, tooÔªø‚Äîit was so unfair, so damned unfair! He remembered Irene saying to him once: ‚ÄúNever was anyone born more loving and lovable than Jon.‚Äù Poor little Jon! His world gone up the spout, all of a summer afternoon! Youth took things so hard! And stirred, tormented by that vision of Youth taking things hard, Jolyon got out of his chair, and went to the window. The boy was nowhere visible. And he passed out. If one could take any help to him nowÔªø‚Äîone must!

He traversed the shrubbery, glanced into the walled gardenÔªø‚Äîno Jon! Nor where the peaches and the apricots were beginning to swell and colour. He passed the Cupressus trees, dark and spiral, into the meadow. Where had the boy got to? Had he rushed down to the coppiceÔªø‚Äîhis old hunting-ground? Jolyon crossed the rows of hay. They would cock it on Monday and be carrying the day after, if rain held off. Often they had crossed this field togetherÔªø‚Äîhand in hand, when Jon was a little chap. Dash it! The golden age was over by the time one was ten! He came to the pond, where flies and gnats were dancing over a bright reedy surface; and on into the coppice. It was cool there, fragrant of larches. Still no Jon! He called. No answer! On the log seat he sat down, nervous, anxious, forgetting his own physical sensations. He had been wrong to let the boy get away with that letter; he ought to have kept him under his eye from the start! Greatly troubled, he got up to retrace his steps. At the farm-buildings he called again, and looked into the dark cow-house. There in the cool, and the scent of vanilla and ammonia, away from flies, the three Alderneys were chewing the quiet cud; just milked, waiting for evening, to be turned out again into the lower field. One turned a lazy head, a lustrous eye; Jolyon could see the slobber on its grey lower lip. He saw everything with passionate clearness, in the agitation of his nervesÔªø‚Äîall that in his time he had adored and tried to paintÔªø‚Äîwonder of light and shade and colour. No wonder the legend put Christ into a mangerÔªø‚Äîwhat more devotional than the eyes and moon-white horns of a chewing cow in the warm dusk! He called again. No answer! And he hurried away out of the coppice, past the pond, up the hill. Oddly ironicalÔªø‚Äînow he came to think of itÔªø‚Äîif Jon had taken the gruel of his discovery down in the coppice where his mother and Bosinney in those old days had made the plunge of acknowledging their love. Where he himself, on the log seat the Sunday morning he came back from Paris, had realised to the full that Irene had become the world to him. That would have been the place for Irony to tear the veil from before the eyes of Irene‚Äôs boy! But he was not here! Where had he got to? One must find the poor chap!

A gleam of sun had come, sharpening to his hurrying senses all the beauty of the afternoon, of the tall trees and lengthening shadows, of the blue, and the white clouds, the scent of the hay, and the cooing of the pigeons; and the flower shapes standing tall. He came to the rosery, and the beauty of the roses in that sudden sunlight seemed to him unearthly. ‚ÄúRose, you Spaniard!‚Äù Wonderful three words! There she had stood by that bush of dark red roses; had stood to read and decide that Jon must know it all! He knew all now! Had she chosen wrong? He bent and sniffed a rose, its petals brushed his nose and trembling lips; nothing so soft as a rose-leaf‚Äôs velvet, except her neckÔªø‚ÄîIrene! On across the lawn he went, up the slope, to the oak-tree. Its top alone was glistening, for the sudden sun was away over the house; the lower shade was thick, blessedly coolÔªø‚Äîhe was greatly overheated. He paused a minute with his hand on the rope of the swingÔªø‚ÄîJolly, HollyÔªø‚ÄîJon! The old swing! And suddenly, he felt horriblyÔªø‚Äîdeadly ill. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve over done it!‚Äù he thought: ‚Äúby Jove! I‚Äôve overdone itÔªø‚Äîafter all!‚Äù He staggered up toward the terrace, dragged himself up the steps, and fell against the wall of the house. He leaned there gasping, his face buried in the honeysuckle that he and she had taken such trouble with that it might sweeten the air which drifted in. Its fragrance mingled with awful pain. ‚ÄúMy love!‚Äù he thought; ‚Äúthe boy!‚Äù And with a great effort he tottered in through the long window, and sank into old Jolyon‚Äôs chair. The book was there, a pencil in it; he caught it up, scribbled a word on the open page.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ His hand dropped.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ So it was like thisÔªø‚Äîwas it?Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶

There was a great wrench; and darkness.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶
